annotations.label: Time complexity

anchor.snippet: the time complexity is the computational complexity that describes the amount of computer time it takes to run an algorithm

annotations.note: Mathlib bundles a Turing machine with a time function (ℕ→ℕ counting steps) and a proof it runs within that bound, but defines no standalone 'time complexity' quantity.

annotations.label: Worst-case time complexity

anchor.snippet: the worst-case time complexity , which is the maximum amount of time required for inputs of a given size

annotations.note: TM2ComputableInTime requires its time bound to hold for all inputs of a given length (a uniform worst-case upper bound), but the worst-case maximum is not defined as a quantity.

annotations.label: Average-case complexity

anchor.snippet: the average-case complexity , which is the average of the time taken on inputs of a given size

annotations.note: No averaging of running time over inputs of a given size is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Linear and polynomial time classification

anchor.snippet: is a linear time algorithm and an algorithm with time complexity

annotations.note: Polynomial-time computability is formalized via TM2ComputableInPolyTime, but linear time has no separate named definition.

annotations.label: Constant time

anchor.snippet: An algorithm is termed constant time

annotations.note: No definition of constant-time computation exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Array element access

anchor.snippet: accessing a specific element in an array is a constant-time operation, as it requires only a single operation to locate that element

annotations.note: Mathlib has no cost model asserting array access is constant time.

annotations.label: Minimum of unordered array

anchor.snippet: determining the minimum value in an unordered array is not constant time; it requires examining each element

annotations.note: No running-time analysis of finding a minimum is formalized.

annotations.label: Swapping values

anchor.snippet: a task that involves swapping the values of a and b to ensure

annotations.note: The constant-time swap example is not a formalized statement in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Logarithmic time

anchor.snippet: An algorithm is said to take logarithmic time when

annotations.note: No definition of logarithmic-time computation exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dictionary search

anchor.snippet: An example of logarithmic time is given by dictionary search.

annotations.note: No binary/dictionary search running-time result is formalized.

annotations.label: Polylogarithmic time

anchor.snippet: An algorithm is said to run in polylogarithmic time if its time

annotations.note: No definition of polylogarithmic-time computation exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Matrix chain ordering

anchor.snippet: matrix chain ordering can be solved in polylogarithmic time on a parallel random-access machine

annotations.note: The matrix chain ordering algorithm and its complexity are not formalized.

annotations.label: Sub-linear time

anchor.snippet: An algorithm is said to run in sub-linear time (often spelled sublinear time ) if

annotations.note: No definition of sub-linear-time computation exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sublinear time algorithm (sampling)

anchor.snippet: The specific term sublinear time algorithm commonly refers to randomized algorithms that sample a small fraction of their inputs

annotations.note: Randomized sublinear sampling algorithms are not formalized.

annotations.label: Settings for sublinear time

anchor.snippet: Other settings where algorithms can run in sublinear time include

annotations.note: No sublinear-time algorithmic settings are formalized.

annotations.label: Linear time

anchor.snippet: An algorithm is said to take linear time

annotations.note: Linear time has no named definition; only the generic time-bounded computability structure exists.

annotations.label: Quasilinear time

anchor.snippet: An algorithm is said to run in quasilinear time (also referred to as log-linear time ) if

annotations.note: No definition of quasilinear-time computation exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Comparison sort lower bound

anchor.snippet: Comparison sorts require at least

annotations.note: The Ω(n log n) comparison-sort lower bound is not formalized in Mathlib's sorting files.

annotations.label: Sub-quadratic time

anchor.snippet: An algorithm is said to be subquadratic time if

annotations.note: No definition of sub-quadratic-time computation exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Polynomial time

anchor.snippet: An algorithm is said to be of polynomial time if its running time is upper bounded by a polynomial expression in the size of the input

annotations.note: TM2ComputableInPolyTime bundles a Turing machine with a `Polynomial ℕ` time function and a proof it outputs the result within `time.eval (input.length)` steps, directly formalizing polynomial-time computation.

annotations.label: Cobham's thesis

anchor.snippet: Cobham's thesis states that polynomial time is a synonym for

annotations.note: Cobham's thesis is a philosophical/empirical claim about tractability and is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Selection sort

anchor.snippet: The selection sort sorting algorithm on n integers performs

annotations.note: Selection sort and its operation count are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Class P

anchor.snippet: P : The complexity class of decision problems that can be solved on a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time

annotations.note: Polynomial-time deterministic computation is formalized, but the complexity class P (a set of decision problems) is not defined.

annotations.label: Class NP

anchor.snippet: NP : The complexity class of decision problems that can be solved on a non-deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time

annotations.note: Mathlib has no nondeterministic Turing machine model or complexity class NP.

annotations.label: Class ZPP

anchor.snippet: ZPP : The complexity class of decision problems that can be solved with zero error on a probabilistic Turing machine in polynomial time

annotations.note: No probabilistic Turing machine model or class ZPP exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Class RP

anchor.snippet: RP : The complexity class of decision problems that can be solved with 1-sided error on a probabilistic Turing machine in polynomial time.

annotations.note: No probabilistic Turing machine model or class RP exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Class BPP

anchor.snippet: BPP : The complexity class of decision problems that can be solved with 2-sided error on a probabilistic Turing machine in polynomial time

annotations.note: No probabilistic Turing machine model or class BPP exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Class BQP

anchor.snippet: BQP : The complexity class of decision problems that can be solved with 2-sided error on a quantum Turing machine in polynomial time

annotations.note: No quantum Turing machine model or class BQP exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: P robust under machine model

anchor.snippet: P is the smallest time-complexity class on a deterministic machine which is robust in terms of machine model changes

annotations.note: No robustness statement about the class P across machine models is formalized.

annotations.label: Superpolynomial time

anchor.snippet: An algorithm is defined to take superpolynomial time if T ( n ) is not bounded above by any polynomial

annotations.note: No definition of superpolynomial-time computation exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: 2^n steps

anchor.snippet: an algorithm that runs for 2 n steps on an input of size n requires superpolynomial time

annotations.note: This example about 2^n-step algorithms is not formalized.

annotations.label: Adleman–Pomerance–Rumely primality test

anchor.snippet: the Adleman–Pomerance–Rumely primality test runs for n O (log log n ) time on n -bit inputs

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes Lucas-Lehmer/Lucas primality but not the APR test or its running time.

annotations.label: Quasi-polynomial time

anchor.snippet: Quasi-polynomial time algorithms are algorithms whose running time exhibits quasi-polynomial growth

annotations.note: No definition of quasi-polynomial-time computation exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Directed Steiner tree problem

anchor.snippet: a famous example is the directed Steiner tree problem

annotations.note: The directed Steiner tree problem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Planted clique problem

anchor.snippet: the planted clique problem in which the goal is to find a large clique in the union of a clique and a random graph

annotations.note: The planted clique problem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Class QP

anchor.snippet: The complexity class QP consists of all problems that have quasi-polynomial time algorithms.

annotations.note: The complexity class QP is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Conjectured lack of sub-exponential algorithms

anchor.snippet: it is conjectured for many natural NP-complete problems that they do not have sub-exponential time algorithms

annotations.note: No conjecture about sub-exponential algorithms for NP-complete problems is formalized.

annotations.label: Sub-exponential time (first definition)

anchor.snippet: A problem is said to be sub-exponential time solvable if it can be solved in running times whose logarithms grow smaller than any given polynomial.

annotations.note: No definition of sub-exponential-time solvability exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sub-exponential time (second definition)

anchor.snippet: Some authors define sub-exponential time as running times in

annotations.note: No definition of sub-exponential time (second form) exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: General number field sieve

anchor.snippet: the best-known classical algorithm for integer factorization, the general number field sieve

annotations.note: The general number field sieve is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Class SUBEPT

anchor.snippet: SUBEPT is the class of all parameterized problems that run in time sub-exponential in k and polynomial in the input size n

annotations.note: The parameterized complexity class SUBEPT is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Exponential time hypothesis

anchor.snippet: The exponential time hypothesis ( ETH ) is that 3SAT , the satisfiability problem of Boolean formulas in conjunctive normal form

annotations.note: The exponential time hypothesis and 3SAT are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: ETH implies P ≠ NP

anchor.snippet: The exponential time hypothesis implies P ≠ NP .

annotations.note: Neither ETH nor P≠NP is formalized, so this implication is absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Exponential time

anchor.snippet: An algorithm is said to be exponential time , if T ( n ) is upper bounded by 2 poly( n )

annotations.note: No definition of exponential-time computation exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Class EXP

anchor.snippet: Problems which admit exponential time algorithms on a deterministic Turing machine form the complexity class known as EXP

annotations.note: The complexity class EXP is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Class E

anchor.snippet: Sometimes, exponential time is used to refer to algorithms that have T ( n ) = 2 O ( n )

annotations.note: The complexity class E is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Factorial time

anchor.snippet: An algorithm is said to be factorial time if T(n) is upper bounded by the factorial function n! .

annotations.note: No definition of factorial-time computation exists in Mathlib (though `Nat.factorial` itself does).

annotations.label: Factorial time ⊂ EXP

anchor.snippet: Factorial time is a subset of exponential time (EXP)

annotations.note: The inclusion of factorial-time problems in EXP is not formalized in Mathlib (neither class is defined).

annotations.label: Bogosort

anchor.snippet: An example of an algorithm that runs in factorial time is bogosort

annotations.note: Bogosort and its complexity are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Double exponential time

anchor.snippet: An algorithm is said to be double exponential time if T ( n ) is upper bounded by 2 2 poly( n )

annotations.note: No definition of double-exponential-time computation exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Class 2-EXPTIME

anchor.snippet: Such algorithms belong to the complexity class 2-EXPTIME .

annotations.note: The complexity class 2-EXPTIME is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Double exponential time algorithms

anchor.snippet: Well-known double exponential time algorithms include

annotations.note: Double-exponential-time algorithm examples are not formalized in Mathlib.
